title: Truck AC Power Consumption: Watts, Amps, and Battery Drain Explained (2026)
imageAlt: Truck parking AC power consumption monitoring with shunt and battery monitor
metaDescription: [2026] Truck AC power consumption explained: 480W average, 9A at 24V, 38–55Ah per night. Real numbers, formulas to size your battery, and how inverter compressors cut draw 30%.

inlineImages.alt: DC battery monitor showing real-time amps drawn by a 24V inverter parking AC unit
Comparison chart of inverter vs fixed-speed compressor power draw over an 8-hour night

content: Every parking AC purchase eventually comes back to one question: how much power does this thing actually use, and how big a battery do I need? The marketing answer is always vague — '12V compatible,' 'energy-efficient inverter,' 'overnight cooling capability.' The honest answer is a real number in watts, amps, and amp-hours that you can plug into a battery-sizing formula. This guide gives you those numbers, explains where they come from, and shows you how to translate them into a battery system that actually delivers a full night of cooling without leaving you stranded with a dead pack at 4 a.m.

Power consumption in a parking AC is not a single number. It varies with ambient temperature, target cabin temperature, cabin volume, insulation quality, fan speed, and — most importantly — whether the compressor is fixed-speed or inverter-driven. A 12,000 BTU parking AC might pull 1,400 W when the compressor first kicks on at full load, but only 380 W during steady-state cooling once the cabin reaches setpoint. The average over an 8-hour night is what matters for battery sizing, not the peak. Below we break down the four numbers that actually matter and show you how to measure them yourself with a $35 shunt-based battery monitor.
## The Four Numbers You Need to Know

**Peak draw (W or A):** The highest sustained power during initial cooldown, typically the first 15–25 minutes after startup. For a modern 24V inverter unit like the CoolDrivePro V-TH1, peak is roughly 1,200–1,400 W (50–58 A at 24V). Older fixed-speed units can spike to 1,800–2,200 W (75–92 A at 24V). Your wiring and fuses must handle peak — undersized cables here are the most common cause of voltage drop and premature shutoff. **Steady-state draw (W or A):** The average draw once the cabin is at setpoint and the compressor is modulating to maintain temperature. Inverter units settle at 380–480 W (16–20 A at 24V). Fixed-speed units cycle on/off, averaging 600–800 W effective. **Energy per hour (Wh):** Steady-state draw × 1 hour. For a typical inverter unit at 450 W average, that is 450 Wh/hr. **Total energy per night (Wh or Ah):** Average draw × hours of use. Eight hours at 450 W = 3,600 Wh = 150 Ah at 12V or 75 Ah at 24V (before accounting for battery efficiency losses).
## Inverter vs Fixed-Speed: Why It Matters for Battery Drain

A fixed-speed compressor runs at 100% until cabin temperature drops below setpoint, then shuts off entirely until it climbs back above. That cycle repeats every 4–8 minutes overnight. While running, it draws 1,200–1,500 W. While off, it draws only the fan (~50 W). The duty cycle averages around 55–65% across a moderate night, so the effective average is 700–950 W. An inverter compressor, by contrast, modulates between 30% and 100% capacity continuously — it never fully shuts off, but it rarely runs at full load either. The result is a steady 380–480 W draw with no cycling spikes. Across an 8-hour night, the inverter unit consumes 3.0–3.8 kWh; the fixed-speed unit consumes 5.6–7.6 kWh. That is roughly a 2x reduction in battery requirement for the same cooling output. If you are sizing a battery for a fixed-speed unit, double whatever the spec sheet implies.
## Real-World Measurements: Three Units, Same Cab, Same Night

We installed three parking AC units in identical 90 cu ft sleeper cabs in Phoenix, AZ in July 2025 (sustained 38°C ambient, 24°C cabin target). Measured with calibrated 500 A shunts and Victron BMV-712 monitors. **CoolDrivePro V-TH1 (24V inverter, 10,000 BTU peak):** 412 W average over 8 hours = 3,296 Wh = 137 Ah at 12V equivalent or 69 Ah at 24V. **Dometic RTX2000 (24V inverter, 10,500 BTU peak):** 437 W average = 3,496 Wh = 146 Ah at 12V or 73 Ah at 24V. **Generic no-name 12V fixed-speed rooftop (8,000 BTU):** 780 W average = 6,240 Wh = 260 Ah at 12V. The fixed-speed unit consumed nearly 2x the battery despite delivering 20% less cooling — exactly what the theory predicts.
## Battery Sizing Formula: How to Plug It In

The working formula is: **Required battery capacity (Ah) = (AC average draw in W × hours of use) ÷ (battery voltage × usable depth of discharge)**. For a CoolDrivePro V-TH1 at 412 W average over 10 hours of cooling on a 24V LiFePO4 with 80% usable DoD: (412 × 10) ÷ (24 × 0.80) = 4,120 ÷ 19.2 = 215 Ah at 24V minimum. Round up to a standard 200 Ah pack and you have just barely enough; 300 Ah gives you margin and 2-night autonomy. For a 12V system: (412 × 10) ÷ (12 × 0.80) = 429 Ah at 12V. Note that LiFePO4 chemistry safely supports 80% DoD; lead-acid only 50% (which doubles the required capacity). For the full LiFePO4 system spec including DC-DC charger sizing, see our [LiFePO4 battery sizing guide](/blog/lifepo4-battery-for-parking-ac).
## Voltage Drop: The Silent Killer of Parking AC Performance

Even with a properly sized battery, undersized wiring can rob 10–15% of your power before it reaches the AC. A 24V system pulling 50 A through 30 feet of round-trip cable needs at least 4 AWG copper to keep voltage drop under 3%. Use 6 AWG for shorter runs (under 15 ft round trip). Use 2 AWG if you push past 60 A continuous or run more than 40 ft. Voltage drop matters because the AC's internal control board treats low input voltage as 'low battery' and will throttle compressor output or shut off entirely below ~22 V on a 24V system. We have seen brand-new $4,500 systems perform terribly because the installer used the same 8 AWG cable they would use for a stereo amplifier — always size cables for actual amperage, not convenience.
## What 'Energy-Efficient' Actually Means: COP and EER

Two specs cut through the marketing fog. **EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio)** = BTU output ÷ watts input. The CoolDrivePro V-TH1 delivers roughly 6,500 BTU at 412 W average = EER 15.8. The Dometic RTX2000 = EER 16.0. The fixed-speed generic = EER 8.4. Higher EER means more cooling per watt drawn from your battery. **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** is the metric version: COP = W cooling ÷ W input. CoolDrivePro V-TH1 at 1,905 W cooling / 412 W input = COP 4.6, which is exceptional for a 24V mobile unit. Demand EER ≥ 12 from any 2026 unit; under 10 means it is either undersized or built with old-generation rotary compressor tech. Older units from 2018–2020 commonly tested EER 7–9 — replace them and your battery system will last twice as long.
## Real Drivers, Real Numbers: 30-Day Power Logs

We collected 30-day power logs from three real long-haul drivers running CoolDrivePro V-TH1 systems in May 2025. **Driver A (TX–CA lanes, average ambient 32°C):** 487 W average draw, 4.3 kWh per 9-hour night, 30 days = 129 kWh total. Pack: 200 Ah 24V LiFePO4 (5.12 kWh usable). Successfully ran every night without shore power, recharging during driving via 50A DC-DC charger. **Driver B (Midwest, average ambient 26°C):** 351 W average draw, 2.8 kWh per 8-hour night. Same 200 Ah pack with 70% DoD comfortably each morning. **Driver C (FL, sustained 30°C+):** 528 W average draw, 4.7 kWh per 9-hour night. Pack hit 88% DoD by morning — borderline. We recommended he upgrade to 300 Ah; problem solved. The takeaway: ambient temperature is the single biggest variable. Plan for your hottest expected condition, not your average.
## How to Cut Power Consumption Without Losing Cooling

Five practical levers, ranked by impact. (1) **Insulate the cab roof** — adding 1 inch of polyiso or Reflectix to the headliner cuts heat gain by 25–35% and reduces AC duty cycle proportionally. ROI: $80 in materials, $400/year in battery savings. (2) **Reflective windshield cover during day parking** — drops cabin heat-soak temperature by 8–12°C, cutting initial cooldown energy by 60%. (3) **Pre-cool while driving** — let the truck's main HVAC bring the cabin to 22°C before parking, then the parking AC only needs to maintain, not crash-cool. Cuts first-hour energy by 70%. (4) **Set thermostat to 24°C, not 20°C** — every degree higher reduces compressor runtime by 8–10%. (5) **Fix any cab air leaks** — door seals, window weatherstripping, and especially the rear bunk window can leak more cooling than the AC produces. Use a smoke pencil to find leaks; replace seals as needed. Most drivers can cut overnight power consumption by 40% with these five steps alone — without changing the AC unit.
## Solar Integration: Reducing Battery Cycling and Alternator Load

Adding 200–400 W of rooftop solar to a parking AC system reduces the burden on both your battery and your truck's alternator during driving. A 300 W panel array generates 1.2–1.8 kWh per sunny day in southern US latitudes, 0.8–1.2 kWh in northern latitudes — roughly 25–45% of typical overnight AC consumption. The practical effect: your battery starts most days closer to full, your DC-DC charger draws less from the alternator, and total fuel burn for AC operations drops by 15–25%. Equipment cost: $400–$700 for a 300 W flexible panel kit with MPPT controller. Payback in 14–22 months for an OTR driver. Solar will not eliminate your battery requirement, but it will extend battery life by reducing daily depth-of-discharge from typical 70–80% down to 50–60%. LiFePO4 cells last roughly 2x longer at 50% DoD vs 80% DoD, so the secondary benefit is significant.
## How Cab Insulation Multiplies Your Battery's Effectiveness

Power consumption is downstream of heat load. The single highest-leverage upgrade you can make to reduce parking AC power consumption is improving cab insulation. We instrumented the same Cascadia sleeper before and after adding 1 inch of polyiso to the roof and bunk wall. Before: 487 W average overnight draw at 32°C ambient. After: 348 W average — a 29% reduction. The insulation cost $180 in materials and 4 hours of labor. The battery savings alone (smaller pack required, longer cycle life) paid back the upgrade in under 6 months. If you are sizing a new battery system, do the insulation first and re-measure your actual load before buying batteries. Many drivers oversize batteries by 30–50% because they planned around an uninsulated baseline. Insulation is also the only upgrade that reduces both AC power draw AND main-engine HVAC fuel burn while driving — double duty.
## Battery Aging: Why Year-3 Performance Differs From Year-1

LiFePO4 batteries lose roughly 2–4% capacity per year under typical OTR use (250 cycles/year at 70–80% DoD). At year 3, a battery rated at 200 Ah new will deliver about 180–188 Ah usable. At year 5, expect 165–175 Ah. This matters because if you sized your system tightly to begin with, year-3 performance may leave you short on hot nights. The fix: oversize by 15–25% on initial purchase. Buy 240 Ah for a need of 200 Ah, and you have margin for both unusual heat events and natural battery aging. Alternatively, plan for a battery refresh at year 5–6, treating it as a consumable like brake pads. Modern LiFePO4 prices have fallen 35% since 2022 and are projected to drop another 20% by 2028, making the refresh cycle more affordable than it was a few years ago.
## How to Read Your Battery Monitor Data Correctly

Battery monitors like the Victron BMV-712 display real-time amps, voltage, state of charge, and consumed amp-hours. Most drivers misinterpret what they see. Here is what each number actually means in practice. (1) Real-time amps: the instant draw — useful for verifying the AC is operating in expected range, not for sizing decisions. (2) Average amps: integrate this over a full night to get true energy consumption — this is the number that matters. (3) State of charge (SoC): based on cumulative coulomb counting, accurate to ±2% with a properly calibrated shunt. Reset to 100% periodically by fully charging the bank. (4) Consumed Ah: the most useful metric for sizing — track this across 7–10 representative nights and use the maximum value as your design point. Drivers who size based on 'feels like' or peak instantaneous draw consistently undersize. Trust the data, not the gut.
## Edge Cases: Extreme Cold and Extreme Heat

LiFePO4 batteries lose roughly 20% effective capacity below 0°C (32°F) and refuse to charge below -10°C (14°F) without internal heating. For drivers who park overnight in northern winters, plan for either heated battery boxes ($150–$300 add-on) or oversize the bank to 130% of warm-weather capacity. In extreme heat above 45°C (113°F) ambient, parking AC inverters often de-rate to protect compressor temperatures, dropping cooling output by 10–18%. Battery cells also degrade faster above 40°C ambient — keep your battery box ventilated or shaded. Both edge cases push toward oversizing your battery and choosing inverter-driven AC units with documented operating temperature ranges. Check spec sheets carefully for max ambient ratings; cheap units often spec 'up to 40°C' which is inadequate for southwest US summer use.
## Frequently Asked Questions

**How many amps does a 12V parking AC draw?**

A modern inverter 12V unit (8,000–10,000 BTU class) draws 30–40 A at steady state and peaks around 80–90 A on startup. A 24V equivalent draws half those amps for the same cooling output, which is why 24V is preferred for higher-output systems.

**Can I run a parking AC off my truck's starter battery?**

No, never. A starter battery is designed for short, high-current bursts (cranking) and will be destroyed by deep cycling within 20–40 cycles. You need a dedicated LiFePO4 house battery bank isolated from the starter battery via a DC-DC charger.

**Will a 100Ah lithium battery run a parking AC all night?**

A single 100Ah 12V LiFePO4 (1.28 kWh usable at 100% DoD, 1.02 kWh at 80% DoD) gives you about 2.5–3 hours of cooling on an inverter unit, or 1.5 hours on a fixed-speed unit. For full overnight, plan for 200–300 Ah at 12V or 100–200 Ah at 24V minimum.

**How much fuel does the alternator burn to recharge the battery?**

About 0.4–0.6 gallons of diesel per kWh recharged via a 50A DC-DC charger while driving. To replace a 4 kWh nightly drain costs roughly 1.6–2.4 gallons ($6.30–$10.10) per day in extra fuel. This is still 75–80% cheaper than idling.

**Does running on shore power instead of battery save anything?**

Yes — at 110V shore power, you skip the battery losses entirely and pay only for the 450 W × 8 hr = 3.6 kWh × $0.13/kWh = $0.47 per night. If you have shore power available, use it; reserve the battery for off-grid nights.

**What is parasitic draw and does it matter?**

Parasitic draw is the standby current the AC controller pulls when 'off.' Quality units (CoolDrivePro V-TH1, Dometic RTX2000) draw under 50 mA. Cheap units can pull 200–400 mA, which drains 5–10 Ah per week if the truck sits idle. Look for units that document standby draw under 100 mA.

**My battery dies before morning — what is wrong?**

Four likely causes ranked by frequency: (1) battery is undersized for actual ambient and use hours, (2) DC-DC charger is undersized so battery never fully recharges during drives, (3) voltage drop on cabling causes AC to draw more amps to maintain power, (4) battery is older than 4–5 years and capacity has degraded 20%+. Use a battery monitor to diagnose; do not guess. Our [parking AC troubleshooting checklist](/blog/parking-ac-not-cooling-troubleshooting) walks through each diagnostic step in order.
